Boot Environment: Complete, bootable ZFS snapshot of an operating system installation. Allows rolling back to a previous system state by selecting a different boot environment at boot time.
SRE: Site Reliability Engineering. Discipline pioneered at Google that applies software engineering practices to operations. SREs define and monitor Service Level Objectives and manage error budgets.
FinOps: Financial Operations. Discipline combining finance and engineering to manage and optimise cloud spend, bringing financial accountability to variable-cost cloud infrastructure.
chroot: A Unix system call that changes the apparent root directory for a process, providing the earliest form of filesystem isolation (1979). The conceptual ancestor of containers and jails.
LXC: Linux Containers. A containerisation runtime using namespaces and cgroups, predating Docker by five years. Used as Docker's original backend before libcontainer.
Alpine Linux: A minimal Linux distribution based on musl libc and BusyBox. Widely used as a Docker base image to reduce image size. Its musl libc can break native Node.js modules that assume glibc.
Oklab: A perceptual colour space published by Bjorn Ottosson in 2020, designed to match human vision uniformly across all hues. The Cartesian form from which OKLCH is derived.
sRGB: Standard RGB. The colour space defined in 1996 for CRT monitors, used as the baseline for HEX and RGB values in CSS. Modern wide-gamut displays exceed its range.
Display P3: A wide-gamut colour space used by modern Apple and Samsung displays, offering roughly 50% more colours than sRGB. Addressable in CSS via OKLCH but not via HEX or RGB.
HSL: Hue, Saturation, Lightness. A CSS colour model expressing colours in human-intuitive terms but with a mathematical lightness value that does not match perceived brightness across hues.
p99 Latency: The 99th-percentile response time, indicating the worst latency experienced by 1% of requests. The standard metric for tail latency in distributed systems.
CSS Grid: CSS layout module for two-dimensional layout, controlling both rows and columns simultaneously. Supersedes many use cases that previously required float hacks or Flexbox workarounds.
Flexbox: CSS layout module for one-dimensional alignment along a single axis (row or column). Universally supported since 2015. Often misused for two-dimensional layouts where CSS Grid is the correct tool.
Procedural Generation: Creating content (geometry, textures, music) algorithmically at runtime from mathematical rules rather than storing pre-authored assets. The technique that makes 64 KB demoscene productions possible.
Raymarching: A rendering technique that traces rays through a scene by iteratively stepping along a signed distance function. Enables full 3D rendering without triangle meshes. Widely used in demoscene intros.
SDF: Signed Distance Function. A mathematical function returning the distance from any point to the nearest surface. Used in demoscene intros to define complex geometry in bytes rather than megabytes of mesh data.
Razor 1911: Norwegian demoscene group founded in 1985, one of the oldest surviving groups. Originated as a cracking group on the Amiga, later transitioned to demo production spanning four decades.
Conspiracy: Hungarian demoscene group responsible for Clean Slate (2021) and Chaos Theory (2006). Known for technically extreme 64K productions using their in-house apEx demo engine.
Prismbeings: Demoscene group that created Zetsubo (2018, Revision), a 4 KB intro featuring raymarched procedural geometry and a software-synthesised atmospheric soundtrack.
JSDoc: JavaScript documentation standard using comment blocks with @param and @returns tags. With @type annotations, provides full IDE type support via the TypeScript language server without a build step.
esbuild: JavaScript and TypeScript bundler written in Go. 10-100x faster than Webpack by avoiding AST serialisation overhead. Created by Evan Wallace.
OpenSSH: The open-source implementation of the SSH protocol suite, maintained by the OpenBSD project. First released in 1999 by Theo de Raadt. Ships with virtually every Unix-like operating system.
Jenkins: Open-source automation server widely used for CI/CD, configured via Groovy pipelines. The archetypal legacy CI system, largely superseded by GitHub Actions and GitLab CI.
GitHub Actions: GitHub's integrated CI/CD platform using YAML workflow files and a marketplace of reusable actions. Dominant in open-source projects since 2019.
JBIG2: A compression standard for bi-level images using pattern matching. Central to the Xerox scanner bug where a scanner silently substituted digits in scanned documents by matching similar-looking number shapes.
Normalisation: Organising a relational database schema to reduce data redundancy and improve integrity by applying normal forms (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF). The discipline MongoDB told you to skip.
:has(): CSS relational pseudo-class (Baseline 2023) that selects an element based on what it contains, enabling parent selection for the first time in CSS history. Replaces JavaScript for state-dependent parent styling.
:is(): CSS pseudo-class that groups multiple selectors into one, adopting the specificity of its most specific argument. Eliminates selector repetition.
:where(): CSS pseudo-class with identical grouping behaviour to :is() but with always-zero specificity. Ideal for defaults and reset layers that should never win a specificity contest.
RFC 6455: The IETF standard defining the WebSocket protocol (2011). Specifies the HTTP upgrade handshake, frame format (2-byte minimum header), ping/pong heartbeats, and close semantics for persistent full-duplex communication.
Socket.IO: A JavaScript library for real-time communication between browser and server. 46 KB client bundle plus transitive npm dependencies. Provides fallbacks and reconnection logic that the native WebSocket API does not need.
Long Polling: A technique where the client sends an HTTP request and the server holds it open until data is available, then responds and the client immediately reconnects. A 2006 workaround for the lack of persistent connections, superseded by WebSocket.
Full-Duplex: A communication mode where both endpoints can send and receive data simultaneously over the same channel. WebSocket is full-duplex; HTTP request-response is half-duplex.
SSE: Server-Sent Events. A browser API for one-way real-time communication from server to client over plain HTTP. Auto-reconnect built into the specification. Three headers. No upgrade handshake. 97% browser support since 2015.
EventSource: The browser API for receiving Server-Sent Events. One line to connect, automatic reconnection, Last-Event-ID for resumption. The specification demands reconnect behaviour, not the developer.
axum: A Rust web framework built on top of tokio and hyper. Provides ergonomic routing, middleware, and WebSocket support with two crate dependencies. Maintained by the tokio team.
gorilla/websocket: A Go package implementing the WebSocket protocol. Archived but stable. The de facto standard for WebSocket servers in Go before the standard library gained its own implementation.
0b5vr: Japanese demoscener and creative coder. Created 0mix (Revision 2023), a 64 KB browser-based techno live set that won Best Soundtrack at The Meteoriks 2024. Builds custom WebGL frameworks. Source code published on GitHub.
The Meteoriks: The demoscene's annual awards ceremony, recognising outstanding productions across categories including Best Demo, Best Intro, Best Soundtrack, and Outstanding Concept. The demoscene equivalent of the Academy Awards.
Revision: The world's largest demoparty, held annually in Saarbruecken, Germany. Draws over 1,000 sceners from across Europe for competitions in demos, intros, graphics, music, and wild categories.
Algorave: A movement combining live coding and electronic dance music. Performers write synthesis and sequencing code on stage, projected for the audience. The code is the instrument.
GLSL: OpenGL Shading Language. A C-like language for programming GPU shaders. Used in demoscene productions to generate visuals and audio from mathematical functions in real time.
WebGL: A JavaScript API for rendering 2D and 3D graphics in the browser using the GPU. Based on OpenGL ES. Runs without plugins in every modern browser.
FM Synthesis: Frequency Modulation synthesis. A sound generation technique where one oscillator modulates the frequency of another, producing complex timbres from simple waveforms. Used in the Yamaha DX7 and in demoscene audio engines.
Robert C. Martin: Software engineer and author. Published Clean Code (2008) and the SOLID principles. Influential in the Agile movement. His prescriptions for Java enterprise codebases were widely adopted, often without the original context.
Clean Code: A 2008 book by Robert C. Martin prescribing small functions, minimal arguments, and relentless extraction. Written for Java enterprise monoliths with no structure. Widely adopted as universal law regardless of language or context.
FitNesse: An open-source acceptance testing framework co-developed by Robert C. Martin. The chapter 3 refactoring example in Clean Code uses a FitNesse module to demonstrate function extraction.
Dan Abramov: Software engineer, co-creator of Redux and member of the React core team at Meta. Wrote 'Goodbye, Clean Code' (2020), reflecting on the dangers of over-zealous refactoring and premature abstraction.
Lean Code: Vivian Voss's coding methodology: one function, one job, readable at first glance. Prioritises clarity through simplicity over line-count rules. KISS in your head, not rules on a wall.
Jira: A project tracking tool by Atlassian, originally a bug tracker (2002). Grew into a workflow engine, compliance surface, and reporting layer. 68% of new users report feeling overwhelmed in their first month.
Atlassian: Australian software company founded in 2002. Makes Jira, Confluence, Bitbucket, and Trello. Revenue $5.2 billion (FY2025). Killed Jira Server in February 2024, forcing Cloud or Data Center migration.
JQL: Jira Query Language. A proprietary query syntax that resembles SQL closely enough to mislead, then diverges: no joins, no subqueries, useful in one product.
Linear: A project tracking tool built by engineers who left Uber. $1.25 billion valuation, 100 employees, $35,000 total marketing spend. Loads in under one second. The product is the marketing.
Karri Saarinen: Co-founder and CEO of Linear. Previously design lead at Airbnb and Coinbase. Left Uber partly due to frustration with Jira's complexity and built Linear as the alternative.
JetBrains Space: A collaborative development platform by JetBrains integrating project management, code hosting, and CI/CD with IDE support. Launched 2020, discontinued 2024. A better product that lost to inertia.
Early Access: A distribution model where unfinished software or games are sold to fund development. Players pay a reduced price, receive an incomplete product, and provide feedback. Formalised on Steam in March 2013 with twelve titles.
Markus Persson: Swedish game developer, known as Notch. Created Minecraft in 2009, sold the alpha for ten euros with no publisher. Four million copies before release. Sold Mojang to Microsoft for $2.5 billion in 2014.
Minecraft: A sandbox video game created by Markus Persson in 2009. Alpha sold for ten euros, released November 2011. Over 300 million copies sold, making it the best-selling video game in history. The original early access success story.
Supergiant Games: American indie game studio. Developed Bastion, Transistor, Pyre, and Hades. Sold 700,000 copies of Hades during early access, redesigned the narrative around player feedback, and shipped a Game of the Year.
Hades: A roguelike action game by Supergiant Games. Entered early access in December 2018, sold 700,000 copies during EA, and won Game of the Year 2020. Its sequel Hades II sold two million copies in early access.
Larian Studios: Belgian game developer founded in 1996. Spent three years developing Baldur's Gate 3 in early access, sold 2.5 million copies before launch, and shipped a game that won six Game of the Year awards.
Baldur's Gate 3: A role-playing game by Larian Studios. Entered early access in October 2020, sold 2.5 million copies during EA, fully released August 2023. Six GOTY awards. Swen Vincke said it would never get made without early access.
Swen Vincke: Belgian game designer, founder and CEO of Larian Studios. Led the development of the Divinity series and Baldur's Gate 3. Publicly stated that the game would never have been made without early access funding and feedback.
Kerbal Space Program: An orbital mechanics simulator by Squad, a Mexican indie studio. Entered Steam Early Access in March 2013, released April 2015. Endorsed by NASA for its physics accuracy.
CD Projekt Red: Polish game developer. Created The Witcher series and Cyberpunk 2077. Shipped Cyberpunk in December 2020 with critical bugs, causing Sony to delist it and $1.8 billion in market value loss.
Cyberpunk 2077: An open-world RPG by CD Projekt Red. Shipped December 2020 with severe bugs. 13 million copies in ten days. Sony delisted it. Full refunds offered. $1.8 billion in market cap evaporated. A full-price early access title that never called itself one.
Fntastic: Game studio behind The Day Before (2023). Promised an open-world MMO, delivered a broken extraction shooter. 18.5% positive reviews. Studio closed four days after launch. 91,000 of 201,000 copies refunded.
Steam Early Access: A programme launched by Valve on 20 March 2013 with twelve titles. Allows developers to sell unfinished games on Steam. Only 25% of Early Access titles reach full release.
Markdown: A lightweight markup language created by John Gruber in 2004. Designed to be readable as plain text and convertible to HTML. 84% admiration score in Stack Overflow 2024 survey.
CommonMark: A formal specification for Markdown syntax, begun in 2014 by John MacFarlane and Jeff Atwood. Provides 500+ conformance tests to eliminate ambiguity in the original Markdown spec.
John Gruber: Creator of Markdown (2004). Published the original specification as a 1,122-line Perl script with the design goal that a Markdown document should be publishable as-is, as plain text.
Mermaid: A JavaScript-based diagramming tool that renders flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ER diagrams, and Gantt charts from Markdown-like text syntax. Supported natively by GitHub, GitLab, and most Markdown editors.
Confluence: Atlassian's team wiki and documentation platform. Stores content in proprietary XML format. Exports lose macros, user mappings, and formatting.
Typora: A Markdown editor with live rendering, Mermaid support, LaTeX, and export to PDF, HTML, and ePub. One-time purchase, not subscription.
ZFSBootMenu: A Linux boot manager that replaces GRUB entirely, providing native ZFS boot environment support. Third-party, unlike FreeBSD's built-in bectl.
zsys: Ubuntu's abandoned ZFS management daemon. Provided automatic snapshots and boot environments. Development ceased in 2021.
bectl: FreeBSD boot environment management tool, in the base system since FreeBSD 12.0 (2018). Creates, lists, and activates ZFS boot environments without third-party packages.
snapper: A tool for managing Btrfs and LVM snapshots on Linux. Used by openSUSE for automatic pre-upgrade snapshots with GRUB rollback support.
Subgrid: CSS Grid feature allowing a child grid to adopt the track sizing of its parent grid. Enables aligned layouts across nested components without JavaScript. Baseline 2023, 93% browser support.
scroll-snap: A CSS feature (scroll-snap-type, scroll-snap-align) that provides native carousel and slider behaviour without JavaScript. The browser handles snapping between items.
man pages: Unix manual pages, the standard documentation format since 1971. Nine sections covering commands, system calls, library functions, file formats, and system administration. Accessible offline via the man command.
Midnight Commander: A dual-panel text-mode file manager for Unix. Written in C by Miguel de Icaza in 1994. 220,000 lines, 12 MB RAM, built-in editor, viewer, diff, FTP, SFTP, and archive browsing.
Norton Commander: A dual-panel file manager for DOS, written by John Socha in 1984. Became so ubiquitous in the Soviet Union that 'Norton' became the Russian word for file manager. Spawned mc, Total Commander, and FAR Manager.
Miguel de Icaza: Mexican software developer. Created Midnight Commander (1994), co-founded GNOME, created Mono (.NET for Linux), and Xamarin (acquired by Microsoft in 2016).
John Socha: Software developer who wrote Norton Commander in 1984 as a PhD student at Cornell. First director of R&D at Peter Norton Computing.
Eugene Roshal: Russian software developer. Created RAR (1993) and FAR Manager (1996). One of the most quietly productive programmers in computing history.
Christian Ghisler: Swiss software developer. Created Total Commander (1993, originally Windows Commander). One man, thirty-three years of shareware development.
ncurses: A library for writing text-based user interfaces in a terminal. Provides cursor movement, window management, colour, and keyboard input. The foundation of TUI applications like mc, htop, and tmux.
FISH: Files transferred over SHell protocol. Used by Midnight Commander to browse remote filesystems over SSH without requiring SCP or SFTP on the target machine. Needs only a basic shell.
Filippo Valsorda: Italian-American cryptographer. Created age (2019), a modern file encryption tool. Previously maintained Go's cryptography libraries at Google.
scp: Secure Copy Protocol. Copies files between hosts over SSH. Part of the OpenSSH suite. Being deprecated in favour of sftp due to protocol limitations.
age: A modern file encryption tool by Filippo Valsorda (2019). Single binary, no configuration, no key servers. Designed as a simpler replacement for GPG's encryption use case.
LLM: Large Language Model. A neural network trained on text data to generate, complete, and reason about natural language. GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama are LLMs.
Smalltalk: An object-oriented programming language created at Xerox PARC in the 1970s. Pioneered GUI concepts, the IDE, and the MVC pattern. Everything is an object, including numbers and classes.
Xerox PARC: Palo Alto Research Center. Xerox's research lab that invented the graphical user interface, Ethernet, laser printing, Smalltalk, and the MVC pattern. Most inventions were commercialised by others.
Ruby on Rails: A web framework created by David Heinemeier Hansson in 2004. Popularised MVC for web development and convention over configuration. Built on Ruby.
content-visibility: A CSS property that defers rendering of off-screen content until the user scrolls to it. Significant performance improvement for long pages, with zero JavaScript.
showModal(): A method on the native HTML dialog element that opens it as a modal with automatic backdrop, focus trapping, and ESC-to-close behaviour. One line of JavaScript replaces entire modal libraries.
prefers-reduced-motion: A CSS media query that detects whether the user has requested minimal animation in their operating system preferences. A basic accessibility courtesy, one media query.
color-scheme: An HTML meta tag and CSS property that tells the browser the page supports light and dark colour schemes. The browser adapts form controls and scrollbars automatically, without JavaScript.
minline.js: A DOM-based templating engine by Vivian Voss, compressed to exactly 1,337 bytes. Part of the Min2Max ecosystem. Includes a dark/light theme wizard.
David Heinemeier Hansson: Danish programmer, creator of Ruby on Rails (2004). Known as DHH. Popularised MVC architecture for web development and the philosophy of convention over configuration.
htop: An interactive process viewer for Unix systems, created by Hisham Muhammad in 2004. Replaced the workflow of top, ps, grep, kill, and renice with a single interactive binary. 12,000 lines of C.
Hisham Muhammad: Brazilian software developer. Created htop (2004), LuaRocks (the Lua package manager), and co-founded GoboLinux. Handed htop to the community in 2020 after sixteen years of solo maintenance.
GoboLinux: A Linux distribution co-founded by Hisham Muhammad that uses an alternative filesystem hierarchy where each program lives in its own directory tree, eliminating the need for a package database.
QNX: A commercial real-time operating system with a microkernel architecture. In 1999, QNX fit a complete POSIX OS with GUI, web browser, and TCP/IP stack on a single 1.44 MB floppy disk. Now runs in 215 million vehicles.
BeOS: A media-focused operating system by Be Inc. Released as a free Personal Edition in 2000, downloaded one million times. Featured pervasive multithreading and boot times in seconds. Acquired by Palm for $11 million, source code lost.
NeXTSTEP: An object-oriented operating system by NeXT (Steve Jobs). Where Tim Berners-Lee wrote the first web browser and id Software built Doom. Interface Builder in 1988. Became the foundation of macOS.
Jean-Louis Gassee: French businessman and engineer. Founded Be Inc. and created BeOS. Previously Apple's president of product development. Steve Jobs wanted Apple to acquire BeOS, but Apple chose NeXT instead.
TIC-80: A fantasy console that deliberately mimics 1980s hardware constraints: 240 by 136 pixel display, 16 colour palette, 256 sprites, 4 audio channels. Programs written in Lua or JavaScript.
Spectrox: A demoscene group. Their production Prelude to Neobyte (2025) placed second at Deadline Berlin, running on the TIC-80 fantasy console with voxel landscapes, copper bar effects, and an underwater endscroller.
Virgill: Jochen Feldkoetter, a demoscene musician composing since the early 1990s. Member of Alcatraz and Haujobb. Created AmigaKlang, a modular synthesiser for Amiga productions.
AmigaKlang: A modular synthesiser for Amiga demoscene productions, created by Virgill (Jochen Feldkoetter). Designed for creating music within the extreme constraints of Amiga hardware.
Olympian: A demoscene coder and graphics artist. Created the code and visuals for Spectrox's Prelude to Neobyte (2025), a TIC-80 fantasy console demo.
Next.js: A React meta-framework by Vercel. Adds server-side rendering, routing, and build tooling on top of React. Ships 87 KB of client runtime (App Router) before application code. Fresh project: 247 MB of node_modules.
Vercel: A cloud platform for frontend deployment, founded by Guillermo Rauch in 2015. Maintains Next.js. The framework's features (next/image, ISR, Middleware) assume Vercel's infrastructure.
hydration: The process where a client-side JavaScript framework downloads its runtime, parses it, and re-renders a server-sent HTML page to attach event handlers and make it interactive. Two rendering passes for one page.
Turbopack: A JavaScript bundler written in Rust by Vercel. Announced October 2022, stable 2025. Replaces Webpack as the default bundler for Next.js development. Created because JavaScript was too slow to bundle JavaScript.
ISR: Incremental Static Regeneration. A Next.js feature that regenerates static pages on demand after deployment. Persists to Vercel's edge storage by default, complicating self-hosting.
OpenNext: An open-source adapter project created by Cloudflare and Netlify to make Next.js deployable outside Vercel. The framework's maintainer would not provide this portability.
Guillermo Rauch: Argentine software developer. CEO of Vercel. Created Socket.IO and Mongoose. Founded Vercel (originally ZEIT) in 2015. Maintains Next.js.
Dennis Ritchie: Co-creator of Unix and inventor of the C programming language at Bell Labs. Co-authored The C Programming Language with Kernighan (1978). Turing Award 1983 with Ken Thompson. National Medal of Technology 1999. Died 12 October 2011.
Brian Kernighan: Bell Labs computer scientist. Named Unix. Co-authored The C Programming Language (K&R C) with Ritchie, and co-created AWK. Professor at Princeton University.
Bill Joy: Co-founder of Sun Microsystems. As a graduate student at Berkeley, wrote vi, the C shell, and contributed TCP/IP networking to BSD Unix. One of the most productive programmers in computing history.
vi: A modal text editor created by Bill Joy in 1976 at UC Berkeley. Designed for a 300 baud modem on an ADM-3A terminal. Commands compose as verb + count + noun. Standardised in POSIX. Ships with every Unix system.
Vim: Vi IMproved. Extended vi clone released by Bram Moolenaar in 1991. 380,000 lines of C. Charityware: users asked to donate to orphans in Uganda. Runs on more machines than any other text editor in history.
Bram Moolenaar: Creator and maintainer of Vim for 32 years (1991 to 2023). Coined 'charityware'. Asked users to donate to ICCF Holland for orphans in Uganda. Died 3 August 2023, aged 62. Christian Brabandt continues his work.
ADM-3A: A 1976 dumb terminal by Lear Siegler. The keyboard that shaped vi: arrow symbols on hjkl, Escape where Tab sits today, tilde on the Home key. The hardware origin of Unix conventions still in use fifty years later.
Multics: Multiplexed Information and Computing Service. A time-sharing operating system begun in 1964 by MIT, General Electric, and Bell Labs. Behind schedule and over budget, it drove Bell Labs to withdraw in 1969, directly leading to Unix.
PDP-7: A minicomputer by Digital Equipment Corporation (1964). 18-bit word size, 9 KB of user memory, $72,000 list price. The machine on which Ken Thompson built the first version of Unix in August 1969.
PDP-11: A 16-bit minicomputer by Digital Equipment Corporation (1970). The machine to which Unix was first ported, and on which it was rewritten in C in 1973. One of the most influential computer architectures.
C: A systems programming language created by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973. Designed to make Unix portable. Evolved from BCPL and B. The second most used programming language in the world, fifty-three years after its creation.
B: A programming language created by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs in 1969. Derived from BCPL. Predecessor to C. Used for early Unix development on the PDP-7.
BCPL: Basic Combined Programming Language, designed by Martin Richards at Cambridge in 1967. Ancestor of B and C. Designed for compiler writing, it established the tradition of small, systems-oriented languages.
Bell Labs: Bell Telephone Laboratories. The research division of AT&T where Unix, C, the transistor, the laser, information theory, and the cosmic microwave background were discovered or invented. Home to Thompson, Ritchie, McIlroy, and Kernighan.
Space Travel: A solar system simulation game written by Ken Thompson on the GE-645 mainframe at Bell Labs. The desire to run it on cheaper hardware led Thompson to build Unix on a PDP-7 in 1969.
consent decree: A 1956 antitrust agreement that prohibited AT&T from selling products outside the telephone business. This forced AT&T to license Unix to universities for nominal fees, effectively seeding it across every computer science department.
USL: Unix System Laboratories. A subsidiary of AT&T that sued BSDi and the University of California in 1992, claiming BSD contained proprietary Unix code. The lawsuit settled in 1994: three of 18,000 files were found problematic. The two years of legal uncertainty gave Linux the window to grow.
386BSD: A free operating system by Bill Jolitz, porting BSD to the Intel 386 architecture. Released in 1992. Maintenance gaps led to the creation of FreeBSD, NetBSD, and OpenBSD as community-driven successors.
Jordan Hubbard: Co-founder of the FreeBSD project in 1993, alongside Nate Williams and Rod Grimes. Managed FreeBSD releases for over a decade. Later Director of Engineering at Apple, working on macOS and Darwin.
David Greenman: Proposed the name FreeBSD on the 386BSD mailing list on 19 June 1993. Co-founder and early contributor to the FreeBSD project.
Darwin: The open-source Unix foundation of macOS, iOS, iPadOS, watchOS, and tvOS. A BSD derivative combining the Mach microkernel with FreeBSD userland. Every Apple device runs Darwin at its core.
pipe: A Unix inter-process communication mechanism implemented as a kernel-managed buffer. One process writes bytes in, another reads them out. Proposed by Douglas McIlroy in 1964, implemented by Ken Thompson overnight in February 1973. The character | is the most enduring composition primitive in computing.
named pipe: A FIFO (first in, first out) special file that acts as a pipe between unrelated processes. Unlike anonymous pipes, named pipes exist in the filesystem and persist until deleted. Added in Unix Version 7 (1979). Created with mkfifo.
stdin: Standard input. File descriptor 0 in Unix. The default input stream for a process. Pipes connect one programme's stdout to another's stdin, enabling composition without temporary files.
stdout: Standard output. File descriptor 1 in Unix. The default output stream for a process. The convention of reading from stdin and writing to stdout, established when McIlroy rewrote /bin in 1973, is what makes Unix tools composable.
Constraint Validation API: A native browser API for form validation, part of the HTML5 specification (W3C Recommendation, October 2014). Provides declarative validation via HTML attributes (required, type, pattern, minlength, maxlength) and programmatic access via the ValidityState interface with eleven boolean properties. 97.54% global browser support.
:user-invalid: A CSS pseudo-class that matches form elements the user has interacted with and left in an invalid state. Unlike :invalid, it does not fire on page load. Supported since Firefox 88 (2021), Safari 16.5 (2023), and Chrome 119 (2023). Resolved the biggest UX complaint against native form validation.
Zod: A TypeScript-first schema validation library. 93 million weekly npm downloads (March 2026). Used for runtime type checking and form validation in React applications. Solves a problem the browser's Constraint Validation API already handles for 95% of forms.
react-hook-form: A React form library for managing form state and validation. 21.6 million weekly npm downloads. Wraps native form elements with React hooks and typically pairs with schema libraries like Zod or Yup.
ValidityState: A browser interface exposing eleven boolean properties on every form element: valueMissing, typeMismatch, patternMismatch, tooShort, tooLong, rangeOverflow, rangeUnderflow, stepMismatch, badInput, customError, and valid. Accessible via element.validity. Ships free with every browser since 2014.
Misko Hevery: Creator of Angular (2010) and Qwik. Published 'Hydration is Pure Overhead' in 2022, arguing that hydration re-executes work the server already completed. Built Qwik's resumability model as the alternative.
Qwik: A JavaScript framework by Misko Hevery that replaces hydration with resumability. The server serialises application state; the client resumes without re-rendering. No second pass, no full JavaScript download on page load.
resumability: An alternative to hydration where the server serialises the application state and the client resumes execution without replaying the render. Pioneered by Qwik. Avoids the double-render cost of traditional hydration.
Askama: A compile-time template engine for Rust, inspired by Jinja2. Templates are type-checked at build time. Zero runtime overhead, zero JavaScript. Used with Axum or Actix for server-side rendering without hydration.
templ: A compiled HTML component library for Go. Templates are Go functions, type-checked by the compiler. No runtime template parsing, no JavaScript required. An alternative to React SSR for Go backends.
Astro: A web framework (2020) that introduced islands architecture: static HTML by default, with JavaScript loaded only for interactive components. Ships zero JavaScript unless explicitly requested. Created by Fred K. Schott.
islands architecture: A web architecture pattern where a page is mostly static HTML with isolated interactive 'islands' that load their own JavaScript independently. Introduced by Astro (2020). Reduces JavaScript payload by hydrating only the components that need interactivity.
D. Richard Hipp: Creator of SQLite (2000). Built it for a U.S. Navy destroyer to eliminate the need for a database administrator. Released it into the public domain with a support commitment until 2050. One of the most consequential software engineers in history by deployment count.
WAL: Write-Ahead Logging. A journaling mode for databases where changes are written to a separate log before being checkpointed into the main file. SQLite's WAL mode (2010) enables unlimited concurrent readers alongside a single writer with zero interference.
Informix: A relational database management system originally developed by Informix Software, now owned by IBM. The database whose DBA availability problem on a U.S. Navy destroyer motivated D. Richard Hipp to create SQLite.
DO-178B: Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification. The avionics safety standard that governs software in aircraft. SQLite is certified under DO-178B for use in the Airbus A350 fly-by-wire system.
Apollo Client: A GraphQL client library for JavaScript, providing normalised caching, state management, and query batching. 43 KB gzipped. Replaces functionality that HTTP caching provides for free with REST.
OpenAPI: A specification for describing REST APIs (formerly Swagger). Enables self-documenting endpoints, typed client generation, and tooling integration. Version 3.0 is the current standard.
N+1 problem: A database query pattern where one initial query triggers N additional queries, one per result row. Common in GraphQL resolvers and ORMs. 25 users with posts becomes 26 queries instead of 2.
Dan Schafer: Co-creator of GraphQL at Facebook (2012) with Lee Byron and Nick Schrock. Led the specification design for the query language.
Nick Schrock: Co-creator of GraphQL at Facebook (2012) with Lee Byron and Dan Schafer. Focused on the server-side implementation and internal adoption at Facebook.
Quite: A demoscene group known for consistent 64K intros featuring architectural spaces rendered through raymarching and signed distance fields. Notable works include qq (2012) and Cdak (with Orange, 2010).
Werkkzeug: A visual node-based tool created by Farbrausch for generating 64K intros. Textures, meshes, animations, post-processing, and music are all procedurally generated from operator graphs. No external assets.
Farbrausch: One of the most influential demoscene groups. Created Werkkzeug (the 64K intro toolchain), .kkrieger (a 96 KB FPS game), and FR-08 (Poem to a Horse). Active from 2000 to 2013.
DiHALT: A Russian demoparty held annually since 2003. Features combined demo/intro competitions where size-limited intros compete against unconstrained productions.
raymarching: A rendering technique that steps along rays and evaluates distance functions to find surfaces. Used in demoscene 64K intros because geometry is defined mathematically rather than stored as polygons, dramatically reducing storage requirements.
signed distance field: A mathematical function that returns the distance from any point in space to the nearest surface. Negative values indicate the point is inside the surface. Used with raymarching to render complex geometry without storing polygon meshes.
METR: Model Evaluation and Threat Research. A research organisation that conducts randomised controlled trials on AI tool effectiveness. Their 2025 study found experienced developers were 19% slower when using AI coding assistants.
GitClear: A code analytics platform that analyses git repositories for code quality metrics. Their 2025 study of 211 million lines of code found AI-assisted code has a 41% higher churn rate than human-written code.
CodeRabbit: An AI-powered code review tool. Their analysis of 470 pull requests found AI-generated code produces 1.7x more issues than human-written code.
code churn: Code that is rewritten or deleted shortly after being committed. High churn indicates low initial quality. GitClear found AI-assisted code has 41% higher churn, meaning it gets rewritten within weeks of being written.
Kagi: A paid search engine ($10/month) with no ads, no tracking, and two self-built crawlers (Teclis, TinyGem). 50,000 subscribers. Founded by Vladimir Prelovac. The incentive is search quality, not ad revenue.
Brave Search: An independent search engine with its own web index built from scratch, the only one outside Big Tech in the western world. 50 million queries per day. No surveillance, no ads. Part of the Brave browser ecosystem.
zero-click search: A search query that ends without the user clicking through to any website. 58.5% of Google searches end this way (SparkToro 2024). AI Overviews push the zero-click rate to 83%.
AI Overviews: Google's AI-generated summary feature that appears above search results. Triggers on 50% of US queries. Reduced organic click-through rate by 61%. Removed from health queries in January 2026 after accuracy failures.
SparkToro: A web analytics company founded by Rand Fishkin. Known for their zero-click search studies measuring how many Google searches end without a click to the open web.
dotenv: An npm package (107M weekly downloads) that reads KEY=VALUE pairs from a .env file into process.env. Replaced by Node.js native --env-file in v20.6.0. Unix has provided environment variables since 1979.
environment variable: A key-value pair passed from a parent process to its children via exec(). Introduced in Version 7 Unix (1979). The standard mechanism for process configuration across all Unix-like systems.
login.conf: FreeBSD's login class capability database. Sets environment variables, resource limits, and authentication methods per user class. Configuration lives in /etc, not in the application repository.
GitGuardian: A secrets detection platform. Their 2024 report found 39 million leaked secrets on GitHub, a 67% increase year over year. Most leaks stem from credentials committed in plaintext files.
HashiCorp Vault: An enterprise secrets management system. Stores, rotates, and audits access to credentials, certificates, and encryption keys. Can be integrated with systemd ExecStartPre or FreeBSD rc.d precmd without application-level SDK dependencies.
tmpfs: A temporary filesystem that exists only in memory. Files written to tmpfs vanish on reboot or unmount. Used for transient secrets: ExecStartPre writes to /run (tmpfs), the service reads it, ExecStopPost deletes it. The secret never touches persistent storage.
Let's Encrypt: A free, automated certificate authority launched in September 2015 by ISRG. 700 million websites, 10 million certificates per day. Made HTTPS free and automatic via the ACME protocol. Founded by Josh Aas, Eric Rescorla, Peter Eckersley, and J. Alex Halderman.
ISRG: Internet Security Research Group. The nonprofit behind Let's Encrypt, incorporated May 2013. Founded by Josh Aas and Eric Rescorla (Mozilla), Peter Eckersley (EFF), and J. Alex Halderman (University of Michigan).
Peter Eckersley: Co-designer of the ACME protocol at the Electronic Frontier Foundation. Co-founded Let's Encrypt. Died September 2022, aged 43. The internet is encrypted in large part because of his work.
Certbot: The official ACME client from the Electronic Frontier Foundation. Automates certificate issuance and renewal from Let's Encrypt in a single command. Replaced a 13-step manual process.
CSR: Certificate Signing Request. A block of encoded data sent to a certificate authority containing the public key and domain information. Before ACME, creating and submitting a CSR was a manual process involving openssl commands and web forms.
Popover API: A native HTML API (Baseline April 2025) for showing and hiding overlay content. One attribute (popover) replaces tooltip/dropdown libraries. Provides top layer rendering, light dismiss, ESC handling, and focus management with zero JavaScript.
CSS Anchor Positioning: A CSS specification (Chrome 125+, Safari 26+) that positions one element relative to another via anchor-name and position-anchor. Includes automatic viewport flipping via position-try-fallbacks. Replaces JavaScript positioning libraries.
Floating UI: A JavaScript positioning library (35 KB) for tooltips, popovers, and dropdowns. Successor to Popper.js. Calculates available viewport space and positions elements accordingly. Being replaced by native CSS Anchor Positioning.
top layer: A browser-managed rendering layer that sits above all other content, including elements with z-index: 9999. Used by the Popover API and the dialog element. Eliminates z-index stacking context conflicts.
light dismiss: A UI pattern where an overlay closes when the user clicks outside it or presses Escape. Built into the Popover API's auto mode. Previously required custom JavaScript event listeners on document.
transition-behavior: A CSS property. The value allow-discrete enables transitions on discrete properties like display (none to block). Lets popovers fade out before disappearing without JavaScript timing logic.
CSV: Comma-Separated Values. A text-based data format where fields are separated by a delimiter (comma, pipe, tab, semicolon). No specification until RFC 4180 in 2005, thirty-three years after first use at IBM in 1972. 60% of enterprises still use it for data exchange.
RFC 4180: The IETF specification for CSV, written by Yakov Shafranovich in 2005. Defines comma as delimiter, CRLF as line ending, and double-quote escaping. Published thirty-three years after the format was already in universal use.
Parquet: A columnar storage format for structured data, developed by Apache. Excellent compression and query performance for analytics workloads. Requires a runtime library to read, unlike CSV which requires only a text editor.
Avro: A row-based data serialisation format from the Apache Hadoop ecosystem. Requires a schema definition and a schema registry. Designed for large-scale data processing, not ad-hoc data exchange.
Ryan Dahl: Creator of Node.js (2009) and Deno (2018). Called node_modules 'an irreparable mistake' at JSConf EU 2018 and built Deno as a secure alternative with built-in TypeScript, URL imports, and sandboxed permissions.
Brendan Eich: Created JavaScript in ten days at Netscape in 1995. Originally designed for form validation and image rollovers. Co-founded Mozilla and later Brave Software.
V8: Google's JavaScript engine, written in C++. Powers Chrome and Node.js. Includes a JIT compiler and garbage collector. The GC pauses cause latency spikes in server workloads.
swc: A Rust-based JavaScript/TypeScript compiler. 96% faster than the official TypeScript compiler (tsc). Transpiles TypeScript but skips type checking, trading safety for speed.
event loop: Node.js's single-threaded concurrency model. Processes I/O callbacks on one thread without blocking. Efficient for I/O-bound workloads (waiting for data). Blocks entirely on CPU-bound work (processing data).
semantic versioning: A versioning scheme (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) where major increments signal breaking changes, minor adds features, and patch fixes bugs. In practice, 44% of npm breaking changes arrive in minor/patch releases.
technical debt: The accumulated cost of shortcuts, outdated dependencies, and deferred maintenance in a codebase. Stripe (2018) measured 33% of developer time spent on tech debt. 13.5 hours per week per developer on inefficiencies.
slopsquatting: A supply chain attack where an attacker registers a package name matching one that AI models consistently hallucinate. When a developer accepts the AI's suggestion, they install the malicious package. Exploits the 19.6% hallucinated package rate.
code hallucination: When an AI model generates code referencing functions, methods, packages, or APIs that do not exist. 19.6% of AI-recommended packages are hallucinated. 43% reappear consistently across re-queries. The AI hallucinates with conviction.
Halstead complexity: A set of software metrics measuring program vocabulary and volume. Higher Halstead complexity correlates with higher LLM failure rates in code generation. Named after Maurice Halstead (1977).
agentic model: An AI coding assistant that reasons about architecture, navigates project structure, and maintains context across files. Distinct from autocomplete tools like Copilot that predict the next token based on the current file.
Alan Kay: Computer scientist, Turing Award laureate, pioneer of object-oriented programming and the GUI. Said in 2004: 'Most undergraduate degrees in computer science these days are basically Java vocational training.'
Edsger Dijkstra: Dutch computer scientist, Turing Award laureate. Known for shortest path algorithm, structured programming, and the observation: 'Computer science is no more about computers than astronomy is about telescopes.'
The Missing Semester: A course launched by MIT CSAIL in 2020 teaching shell tools, version control, and command-line automation. Created because MIT observed its own CS students could not use basic Unix tools like grep.
abstraction ceiling: The point at which a developer cannot debug further because they lack understanding of the layer beneath. Docker users who cannot explain namespaces. Kubernetes operators who cannot configure iptables. The foundation was skipped.
Doug McIlroy: Head of Bell Labs Computing Sciences Research Centre. Inventor of the Unix pipe (1973). Wrote the four foundational principles of Unix philosophy in 1978: do one thing well, expect output to become input, try early, use tools.
Rob Pike: Co-creator of Plan 9, UTF-8, and the Go programming language at Bell Labs and Google. Co-authored The Unix Programming Environment (1984) with Kernighan. Known for: 'The power of a system comes from the relationships among programs.'
Peter Salus: Author of A Quarter Century of Unix (1994). Summarised McIlroy's Unix philosophy in three lines: write programs that do one thing well, work together, and handle text streams as the universal interface.
illumos: Open-source continuation of Sun's OpenSolaris. Carries the SVR4 Unix heritage forward. Home of ZFS, DTrace, and Zones: filesystem integrity, dynamic tracing, and OS virtualisation, each doing one thing well.
NetBSD: A Unix operating system focused on portability. One codebase running on over 60 hardware architectures. Clean abstractions and honest interfaces make the code portable by design.
Devuan: A fork of Debian that preserves 'Init Freedom' by removing the dependency on systemd. Described systemd as 'an intrusive monolith' that violates Unix philosophy.
Bryan Cantrill: Co-creator of DTrace at Sun Microsystems (2003) with Mike Shapiro and Adam Leventhal. Now CEO of Oxide Computer Company. Known for passionate advocacy of systems observability and the quote: 'We had created this system, yet we could not observe it.'
eBPF: Extended Berkeley Packet Filter. A Linux kernel technology inspired by DTrace that allows safe bytecode execution in the kernel for tracing, networking, and security. Higher-level interface: bpftrace. Brendan Gregg wrote the definitive material on both DTrace and eBPF.
Brendan Gregg: Systems performance engineer and author. Wrote the definitive books on DTrace and eBPF. Created flame graphs for CPU profiling visualisation. Former Netflix and now Intel performance architect.
Oxide Computer: A rack-scale computer company founded by Bryan Cantrill. Builds servers with the same philosophy as DTrace: fully observable, fully debuggable, fully understood. Hardware and software designed together.
D language (DTrace): The scripting language for DTrace probes. Deliberately constrained: cannot loop infinitely, cannot allocate memory, cannot crash the kernel. Any valid D programme is guaranteed safe on a production system. Safety is a compile-time invariant.
View Transition API: A browser API for animating between page navigations using CSS. Cross-document support since Chrome 126, Edge 126, Safari 18.2. Three lines of CSS replace SPA routing for page transitions. Zero JavaScript. Progressive enhancement: unsupported browsers see a normal page load.
view-transition-name: A CSS property that names an element for the View Transition API. The browser matches elements across pages by name, not by DOM identity. Snapshots the old position, snapshots the new, and animates between them.
Framer Motion: A React animation library (32 KB minified). Provides spring physics, gesture-driven animations, and page transitions. Valuable for complex interactive animations. For page-to-page transitions, replaced by the native View Transition API.
progressive enhancement: A web design strategy where the baseline experience works for all browsers, and enhanced features are layered on top for supporting browsers. The View Transition API is a textbook example: unsupported browsers see a normal page load, not a broken one.
Graydon Hoare: Creator of Rust (2006). Started the language after his elevator crashed due to a C memory bug. Named it after the rust fungus, 'over-engineered for survival.' Stepped down from the project in 2013. The language is now in the Linux kernel, Windows kernel, and every major cloud provider.
borrow checker: Rust's compile-time system that enforces ownership and borrowing rules. Ensures memory safety without a garbage collector. Rejects code it cannot prove safe. Frustrating during learning, but every rejection is a bug you would have shipped to production.
ownership model: Rust's memory management system. Every value has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is freed deterministically. No garbage collector. No runtime cost. The cost is paid at compile time, not at 3 AM.
traits (Rust): Rust's mechanism for shared behaviour, replacing class inheritance. A type implements trait methods explicitly. No diamond problem, no fragile base class, no six-level hierarchy. Composition over inheritance, enforced by the compiler.
CompTIA: A certification organisation generating $168M/year. Acquired by Thoma Bravo and H.I.G. Capital (private equity) in 2024. The nonprofit kept its tax-exempt status. The certification arm became for-profit. The quiet part, said aloud.
Pearson VUE: The world's largest certification exam delivery company. 21 million exams per year across 20,000 test centres in 180 countries. Contract renewal rate: 99%. Revenue: ~$537M/year.
Foote Partners: An IT workforce research firm tracking skills pay premiums across 3,305 employers since 2007. Finding: non-certified technical skills earn nearly 2% more than certified ones. Certification pay premium at its lowest since 2015.
vendor lock-in: The hidden cost of certification ecosystems. Once a team is certified in technology X, switching to Y means writing off years of investment. Certifications are worthless outside the vendor's ecosystem. That is not a side effect. It is the design.
feature creep: The gradual addition of features beyond a product's original scope until it collapses under its own weight. Notion: notes to email client. Jira: bug tracker to 3,000 plugins. iTunes: killed and split into three apps. The inverse symptom: removing features until users leave.
VLC: An open-source media player since 2001 by VideoLAN. No account. No cloud. No AI. No subscription. Plays everything on everything. Has never tried to send your email. The traffic cone icon has not changed. Proof that restraint is a feature.
CLOUD Act: Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data Act (2018). US law allowing law enforcement to compel US tech companies to hand over data stored anywhere in the world. Conflicts with GDPR Article 48. Your Frankfurt data centre is legally American if AWS runs it.
cloud repatriation: Moving workloads back from public cloud to on-premises or colocation infrastructure. 86% of CIOs plan repatriation (Barclays, highest ever). Driven by cost overruns, sovereignty concerns, and the realisation that most workloads do not need elastic scale.
Gaia-X: A European federated cloud initiative launched to counter US hyperscaler dominance. US providers lobbied their way in and, per Nextcloud's founder, 'flooded it with documents.' Founding member Scaleway withdrew. The initiative failed. The data stayed American.
Hetzner: A German dedicated server and cloud provider. For $1,000/month: 112 cores, 896 GB RAM, 140 TB bandwidth. AWS equivalent: 4 mid-tier instances with no bandwidth. 76% better multi-core performance, 11x more IOPS in independent benchmarks.
37signals: The company behind Basecamp and HEY. Left AWS in 2023. Hardware: $700K, recouped year one. Annual savings: $2M. Five-year projection: $10M+. David Heinemeier Hansson documented the entire process publicly.
periodic: FreeBSD's maintenance framework (1990s). Scripts in /etc/periodic/{daily,weekly,monthly,security}. One config file (periodic.conf). Severity-coded exit codes. Collected daily report via email. Custom task: drop a script into the directory. That is the entire API.
pledge: OpenBSD system call that restricts a process to a declared set of operations (e.g. stdio, rpath, inet). Violations are logged and the process is killed. Reported in the daily maintenance email. Security by declaration, not detection.
unveil: OpenBSD system call that restricts a process's filesystem visibility. After calling unveil, the process can only access paths explicitly declared. Combined with pledge, forms OpenBSD's capability-based security model.
ssh-agent: An authentication agent that holds private keys in memory and signs challenges on behalf of SSH clients. 2,624 lines of C, one file, one Unix domain socket. The key never leaves the process. Written by Tatu Ylonen in 1995. Used by every CI/CD system.
Tatu Ylonen: Finnish researcher who created SSH in 1995 at Helsinki University of Technology after a password-sniffing attack. Wrote ssh-agent as part of the original implementation. Founded SSH Communications Security.
agent forwarding: SSH feature (ssh -A) that allows a remote machine to use your local ssh-agent. The remote sshd tunnels signing requests back through the connection. Risk: root on the remote machine can use the forwarded socket. Mitigations: -c confirmation, ProxyJump, destination constraints.
structuredClone: A web platform API (since 2022) for deep-cloning JavaScript objects. Handles Date, Map, Set, RegExp, ArrayBuffer, Blob, circular references, and 30+ types. Zero KB. Replaces JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) which destroys types, and Lodash cloneDeep (17 KB, unchanged for a decade).
structured clone algorithm: The HTML specification's algorithm for deep-copying objects, used internally by postMessage() and IndexedDB since 2010. Exposed publicly as structuredClone() in 2022 after Surma's WHATWG proposal (2016). Type-aware, cycle-safe, no serialisation round-trip.
Redis: Remote Dictionary Server. An in-memory data structure server created by Salvatore Sanfilippo in 2009, initially to power the LLOOGG real-time analytics tool. Single-threaded by design, using an event loop to process commands sequentially without locks or mutexes. Achieves 1.5M+ operations per second through the elimination of contention rather than parallelism.
LLOOGG: A real-time web analytics startup by Salvatore Sanfilippo (2009), predating Google Analytics real-time features by two years. Its inability to perform fast list operations in MySQL directly motivated the creation of Redis.
epoll: A Linux kernel I/O event notification facility (introduced in Linux 2.5.44, 2002). Allows a single thread to monitor thousands of file descriptors for activity without polling. The mechanism Redis uses on Linux for its event loop, replacing the older select() with O(1) event detection.
kqueue: A BSD kernel event notification interface (FreeBSD 4.1, 2000). A generalised event queue handling file descriptors, signals, timers, and more. The mechanism Redis uses on BSD and macOS for its event loop, providing scalable I/O multiplexing without blocking threads.
AGPLv3: GNU Affero General Public License version 3. A copyleft licence that extends GPLv3 with a network use provision: if you run modified software over a network, you must make the source available to users. Redis 8 returned to open source under AGPLv3 in 2024 after a period under the non-open-source SSPL.
pipelining (Redis): A technique where a client sends multiple commands to Redis without waiting for individual replies, then reads all responses at once. Reduces round-trip latency overhead: without pipelining, 180,000 SET operations per second; with 16 commands pipelined, over 1.5 million. No server-side changes required.
Auth0: An Identity-as-a-Service platform founded in 2013 by Eugenio Pace and Matias Woloski. Provides authentication, authorisation, and user management via SDKs and the universal login hosted page. Acquired by Okta in May 2021 for $6.5 billion in stock, for a company generating roughly $200 million in annual revenue.
Okta: An identity and access management vendor founded in 2009 by Todd McKinnon and Frederic Kerrest. Provides Workforce Identity and (via Auth0) Customer Identity services. Breached in January 2022 (Lapsus$, via Sitel) and October 2023 (support system, via HAR files containing session tokens); by March 2025 the 2023 breach scope expanded to full customer list.
Eugenio Pace: Co-founder and original CEO of Auth0. Argentine software engineer who started the company in 2013 and remained CEO through the Okta acquisition in 2021.
Matias Woloski: Co-founder and CTO of Auth0. Argentine software engineer who designed the original Auth0 architecture alongside Eugenio Pace.
Todd McKinnon: Co-founder and CEO of Okta. Former Salesforce engineering executive. Made his first acquisition offer to Auth0 five months after its 2013 founding; the deal closed eight years later, in May 2021.
Lapsus$: A loosely organised extortion group active from 2021 to 2022, responsible for breaches at Nvidia, Samsung, Microsoft, Okta, and others. Typically compromised third-party support engineers or sub-processors rather than target networks directly.
Sitel: A business process outsourcing provider contracted by Okta as a support sub-processor. A Sitel support engineer's laptop was compromised by Lapsus$ in January 2022, triggering the first major Okta breach disclosure.
MAU: Monthly Active Users. The billing unit used by identity providers such as Auth0 to price their service. In late 2023, Auth0's B2C Essentials per-MAU price rose by 300% in a single announcement.
HAR file: HTTP Archive file. A JSON-formatted log of a browser's network activity, including request and response headers, bodies, and cookies. Routinely submitted by customers to support teams for debugging purposes. The 2023 Okta breach exploited HAR files in the support system that contained live session tokens.
OIDC: OpenID Connect. An authentication protocol layered on top of OAuth 2.0. Defines how clients verify the identity of a user based on authentication performed by an authorisation server, and obtain basic profile information. A genuine standard, unlike the custom Rules and Actions that sit on top of it.
SAML: Security Assertion Markup Language. An XML-based standard for exchanging authentication and authorisation data between an identity provider and a service provider. Predates OIDC and remains the de facto standard in enterprise single sign-on.
argon2: A password hashing function and winner of the 2015 Password Hashing Competition. Memory-hard and parameterised against GPU and ASIC attacks. The recommended default for storing user password hashes. Typically a four-line dependency in any modern stack.
Keycloak: An open-source identity and access management solution from Red Hat. Provides OIDC, SAML, and OAuth 2.0, user federation, social login, and admin UI. Java-based, self-hosted, production-grade for over a decade.
Ory Kratos: An open-source, headless user management and authentication system written in Go. Provides REST APIs for login, registration, account recovery, and MFA without shipping a UI, leaving presentation to the application developer.
Authentik: An open-source identity provider supporting OIDC, SAML, and OAuth 2.0. Often chosen as a self-hosted alternative to Auth0 or Keycloak for its cleaner administrative interface.
FusionAuth: An authentication and user management platform distributed as a single binary. Offers a generous free tier for self-hosted deployments and provides SSO, MFA, and user lifecycle management.
Authelia: An open-source authentication and authorisation server designed as forward auth middleware for reverse proxies such as nginx, Traefik, and Caddy. Lightweight and single-purpose.
Lucia: A lightweight authentication library for Node.js and the Web platform. Unopinionated about sessions and database access, designed to be embedded in the application rather than run as a separate service.
Session token: An opaque, server-issued identifier (commonly a UUID or cryptographically random string) associating an authenticated client with server-side session state. Live tokens stolen via HAR files enabled the 2023 Okta breach lateral movement.
Moore's Law: The observation by Gordon Moore in 1965 that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every two years. Treated for decades as a law of physics, it was always an economic and engineering trend; single-thread performance scaling has been flattening since roughly 2005.
Premature optimisation: A phrase from Donald Knuth's 1974 paper 'Structured Programming with go to Statements'. The full sentence reads: 'We should forget about small efficiencies, say about 97 per cent of the time: premature optimisation is the root of all evil. Yet we should not pass up our opportunities in that critical 3 per cent.' The 3 per cent clause is almost universally omitted.
Erlang: A concurrency-oriented functional programming language developed at Ericsson in 1986 for telecom switches. Lightweight processes, message passing, and supervisor trees. The language behind WhatsApp's ability to serve 900 million users with fifty engineers.
BEAM: Bogdan's Erlang Abstract Machine. The virtual machine that executes Erlang and Elixir code. Scheduler-per-core architecture, preemptive scheduling, and sub-microsecond process creation. Used by WhatsApp, Discord (for chat), and RabbitMQ.
Mnesia: A distributed, real-time database management system built into Erlang/OTP. Tables can be stored in RAM, on disk, or replicated across nodes. Part of the stack that allowed WhatsApp to scale to 900 million users.
ejabberd: An open-source XMPP messaging server written in Erlang. Originally built by Alexey Shchepin in 2002. Used by WhatsApp as the messaging layer alongside Erlang and Mnesia during its 2015 peak efficiency period.
WhatsApp: A messaging application founded in 2009 by Jan Koum and Brian Acton. Served 900 million users with fifty engineers in 2015, on a stack of FreeBSD, Erlang, ejabberd, the BEAM VM, and Mnesia. A ratio of one engineer per 18 million users. Acquired by Facebook in 2014; the FreeBSD stack was later migrated to Linux to fit Facebook's containerised operations.
Discord: A voice, video, and text messaging platform founded in 2015 by Jason Citron and Stanislav Vishnevskiy. In 2020, rewrote its Read States service from Go to Rust; memory dropped 40 per cent, worst-case latency improved 160x. The chat backend runs on Elixir/BEAM.
Read States (Discord): The Discord service that tracks, for every user, the most recent message each channel has been read up to. Originally Go, rewritten to Rust in 2020 to eliminate garbage-collection latency spikes. Memory down 40 per cent, latency 6.5x best case and 160x worst case.
Home Mixer: Twitter's (later X's) recommendation and timeline ranking service, rewritten from Scala to Rust and Python in 2022-2023. Reported to run approximately 10x faster than its predecessor, Timeline Mixer. Part of the infrastructure overhaul that followed the $1 billion cost-cutting target.
IEA: International Energy Agency. Paris-based intergovernmental organisation established in 1974. Publishes the annual 'Energy and AI' reports that track global data centre electricity consumption. Projected 415 TWh in 2024 rising to 945 TWh by 2030.
TWh: Terawatt-hour. One trillion watt-hours, or one billion kilowatt-hours. The unit used for national and global electricity consumption figures. Global data centres consumed 415 TWh in 2024, roughly 1.5 per cent of total worldwide electricity production.
HTTP Archive: A permanent repository of web performance information. Crawls millions of URLs monthly and records their page weight, resource counts, and rendering metrics. Its annual Web Almanac documents the inflation of median page weight from 500 KB (2010) to 2.9 MB (2025).
DoubleClick: An advertising technology company founded in January 1996 in New York by Kevin O'Connor and Dwight Merriman. Introduced the third-party cookie via its DART platform (Dynamic Advertising, Reporting, and Targeting). Acquired Abacus Direct in 1999. Acquired by Google in 2007 for $3.1 billion; the EU approved the acquisition in March 2008.
DART: Dynamic Advertising, Reporting, and Targeting. The original DoubleClick platform, launched in 1996. Introduced the third-party cookie for cross-site tracking. The technical mechanism was mundane; the architectural consequence was the foundation of modern web surveillance.
Third-party cookie: A small text file set by a server the user never directly visited, carried in requests from any site that embeds that server's resources (a pixel, a script, an iframe). The mechanism by which DoubleClick, and its descendants, follow users across the web.
Kevin O'Connor: Co-founder and first CEO of DoubleClick, alongside Dwight Merriman, in January 1996. Left DoubleClick in 2001 to run ScOp Venture Capital, through which he funds early-stage technology companies.
Dwight Merriman: Co-founder and CTO of DoubleClick (1996). Later co-founded MongoDB Inc. in 2007 alongside Eliot Horowitz, applying the scaling lessons of DoubleClick's ad infrastructure to database design.
Abacus Direct: A consumer-purchasing data broker acquired by DoubleClick in 1999. DoubleClick proposed merging offline purchase records with online browsing data, prompting a US Federal Trade Commission investigation in 2000. DoubleClick backed down at the time; Google acquired the combined entity in 2007.
Real-time bidding: The auction protocol used to sell online ad impressions in real time. For each page load, an ad exchange solicits bids from advertisers (each of whom may be given user data), then serves the winning creative. Operates at approximately 600 billion bid requests per day, 6.9 million per second, across the open web.
App Tracking Transparency: An Apple iOS feature introduced in iOS 14.5 in April 2021. Requires apps to obtain the user's permission via a system dialog before tracking them across other companies' apps and websites. Opt-in rates settled at 15 to 25 per cent; Meta's CFO David Wehner estimated 2022 revenue loss at approximately $10 billion.
Consent Management Platform: A third-party service that generates, displays, and records consent for cookie banners on websites. 67 per cent of cookie banners on top sites are served by a CMP; three firms hold 37 per cent of the CMP market between them.
Cookie banner: A dialog shown to a website visitor to collect consent for cookies and similar tracking technologies, primarily driven by the EU's GDPR and ePrivacy Directive. When the 'Reject all' button is hidden behind additional clicks, up to 90 per cent of users accept.
David Wehner: Chief Financial Officer of Meta Platforms (formerly Facebook) from 2014 to 2022. In 2022 he disclosed on an earnings call that Apple's App Tracking Transparency would cost Meta approximately $10 billion in revenue that year.
WhoTracks.me: A public dataset and website maintained by Ghostery and formerly by Cliqz. Crawls the top sites on the web and reports, per site, which third-party trackers are present. Core data source for most external analyses of web tracking prevalence.
Ghostery: A privacy browser extension that blocks third-party trackers and ads. Originally developed in 2009, later acquired by Cliqz, and subsequently relaunched as an independent company. Maintains the WhoTracks.me dataset.
Eliot Horowitz: Co-founder and former CTO of MongoDB Inc. Previously a software engineer at DoubleClick, he launched MongoDB in 2007 alongside Dwight Merriman and Kevin Ryan, applying the scaling lessons of the DoubleClick ad infrastructure to a general-purpose document database.
ScOp Venture Capital: An early-stage venture capital firm founded by Kevin O'Connor after his 2001 departure from DoubleClick. Invests primarily in technology companies, continuing the O'Connor thread through the DoubleClick alumni network.
Prodigy: An early online service operated jointly by IBM, Sears, and CBS, active from 1984 to 2001. One of the first commercial services to cache data on or near users' personal computers to reduce networking and server costs. An early demonstration that any data path optimised for cost eventually becomes commercially interesting.
MongoDB: A document-oriented database released in 2009 by MongoDB Inc., founded in 2007 by former DoubleClick engineers Dwight Merriman, Kevin Ryan, and Eliot Horowitz. Stores JSON-like documents in collections; popular for web applications despite ongoing debates about its consistency guarantees.
zfs snapshot: An atomic, block-level, immutable view of a ZFS dataset at a given instant. Created in milliseconds on active datasets, holds no lock, copies no files. The unit of backup, replication, and branching on ZFS.
zfs send: A ZFS command that serialises a snapshot (or the delta between two snapshots) into a byte stream. Block-level, not file-level. Typically piped over SSH to a remote 'zfs receive' for disaster-recovery replication and read-replica provisioning. Incremental sends of a 40 TB dataset with small deltas complete in seconds.
zfs clone: A writable copy of a ZFS snapshot created with copy-on-write semantics. Costs no additional disk space until the clone diverges from the parent, at which point only the changed blocks are written. The primitive behind Git-like branching on a multi-terabyte filesystem.
zfs diff: A ZFS command that compares two snapshots (or a snapshot and its active dataset) and emits a file-level, path-based list of additions, removals, modifications, and renames. File-level, not block-level.
zfs rollback: A ZFS command that reverts a dataset to the state captured in a named snapshot. Single-command; execution time is measured in the duration of typing it, not in the size of the dataset.
zfs promote: A ZFS command that swaps the parent-child relationship between a clone and its origin dataset. Used when a clone is intended to become the new canonical dataset and the former parent is to be retired.
Copy-on-write: A storage and memory technique where a shared block is not duplicated until one of its users modifies it, at which point only the modifying user pays for a new copy. The basis for ZFS snapshots, clones, and most modern filesystems.
btrfs: A Linux copy-on-write filesystem merged into the mainline kernel in 2009. Offers subvolume snapshots, clones, and send/receive. RAID 5/6 remains officially discouraged for production use in 2026; send requires the source subvolume to be read-only; deduplication is offline only.
Subvolume: A named, independently mountable and snapshottable region of a btrfs filesystem, broadly analogous to a ZFS dataset. Multiple subvolumes share the underlying storage pool.
Paweł Jakub Dawidek: FreeBSD committer who ported OpenSolaris ZFS to FreeBSD in 2007, shipping first in FreeBSD 7.0 (2008). His work brought inline snapshots, copy-on-write clones, and block-level replication to the FreeBSD base system.
sanoid: A ZFS snapshot management policy engine written by Jim Salter. Manages scheduled snapshots, retention, and pruning on top of the zfs snapshot primitive. Typically paired with syncoid for replication.
syncoid: A companion tool to sanoid that automates zfs send and zfs receive, including incremental replication, recursive datasets, and resumable transfers. Reduces the send/receive workflow to a single command.
zrepl: A ZFS replication daemon written in Go. Provides scheduled, resumable replication over SSH or TLS, with pull and push modes and encrypted datasets. Sits above the zfs send and zfs receive primitives.
Aaron Swartz: American programmer and internet activist (1986-2013). Co-author of the RSS 1.0 specification at age fourteen, principal architect of the Creative Commons technical infrastructure, co-founder of Reddit, founder of the web.py framework, and the 'sole beta-tester' whose feedback shaped Markdown's syntax during its 2003-2004 design. Died in January 2013 during federal prosecution for downloading academic papers from JSTOR.
atx: A plain-text shorthand for HTML created by Aaron Swartz in 2002. Introduced the hash-prefix heading convention (#, ##, ###) that was subsequently adopted by Markdown in 2004. Paired with Swartz's html2text, a reverse converter that turns HTML back into atx/Markdown syntax.
html2text: A Python tool by Aaron Swartz that converts HTML into atx/Markdown-style plain text. Released December 2002 as the reverse complement to atx, enabling round-trip editing of HTML in a plain-text editor.
Daring Fireball: John Gruber's long-running personal blog, launched in 2002. The venue where Markdown was announced on 15 March 2004 and where the original reference implementation Markdown.pl was first distributed.
GitHub Flavored Markdown: A Markdown dialect introduced by GitHub in 2009 and formally specified in 2017. Extends CommonMark with tables, task lists, strikethrough, and autolinks. The flavour most developers actually mean when they say 'Markdown'.
John MacFarlane: Philosopher at the University of California, Berkeley and author of Pandoc, the universal document converter. Co-led the CommonMark effort with Jeff Atwood from 2012, producing the first formal Markdown specification in 2014.
Jeff Atwood: Software developer and co-founder of Stack Overflow (2008) and Discourse (2013). Co-led the CommonMark standardisation effort with John MacFarlane from 2012 and authored the 'Coding Horror' blog.
Pandoc: A universal document converter written by John MacFarlane, first released in 2006. Translates between dozens of markup formats (Markdown, reStructuredText, HTML, LaTeX, DOCX, EPUB, and many more). The reference implementation for most of the extensions that CommonMark later absorbed.
Discourse: An open-source internet forum platform released in 2013 by Jeff Atwood, Robin Ward, and Sam Saffron. Uses CommonMark-based Markdown for post authoring and was one of the early drivers of the CommonMark standardisation effort.
Notion: A note-taking, knowledge management, and collaboration application launched in 2016. Stores block-structured content internally but exports and imports in Markdown; widely credited with popularising Markdown-native personal knowledge management in the mainstream market.
Obsidian: A local-first personal knowledge management application released in 2020. Stores notes as plain Markdown files on disk with a graph view over internal links. Popular in the Zettelkasten and second-brain communities.
Logseq: An open-source, block-based, Markdown-native personal knowledge management application. Combines outlining with backlinks and graph views, storing all content as Markdown files in a local folder.
web.py: A minimalist Python web framework written by Aaron Swartz, first released in 2006. Provided the foundation for early Reddit and a number of other small-to-mid-scale web applications of the period.
@scope: A CSS at-rule that fences a block of style rules to a region of the DOM bounded by an upper scope root and an optional lower scope limit. Shipped in Chrome 118 (October 2023), Safari 17.4 (March 2024), and Firefox 146 (early 2026); reached Baseline 'Newly available' status in December 2025 with approximately 91 per cent global usage.
Scoping proximity: A sorting tier introduced to the CSS cascade by CSS Cascade Level 6. When two @scope blocks produce conflicting declarations for the same element, the rule whose scope root is fewer DOM hops away from that element wins. Sits between specificity and source order in the cascade order.
Donut scope: The informal name for the @scope (root) to (limit) pattern: style the doughnut (everything inside the root), leave the hole alone (everything inside the limit). Borrowed from the Oddbird @scope explainer by Miriam Suzanne.
:scope: A CSS pseudo-class that, inside an @scope block, refers to the current scope root. Allows rules to target the root itself, for example 'padding' or 'border' on the component container.
:where(): A CSS pseudo-class functional notation that takes a selector list and matches any of those selectors, with zero specificity. Used in the @scope specification to explain how bare selectors inside a scope retain their original specificity.
CSS Cascade Level 6: The current W3C Working Draft that formalises @layer, @scope, and scoping proximity as part of the CSS cascade. Revises the cascade order to: importance, cascade layers, specificity, scoping proximity, source order.
Miriam Suzanne: CSS specification editor, member of the CSS Working Group, and co-founder of Oddbird. Authored the current @scope explainer and has co-edited specifications including Cascade Layers and Container Queries.
Oddbird: A web development consultancy and CSS specification contributor co-founded by Miriam Suzanne. Host of the public @scope explainer and a long-running source of upstream CSS work.
CSS Modules: A JavaScript-based scoping approach that hashes CSS class names at build time to produce locally-scoped class identifiers. Solves the global-namespace problem at the cost of a build step and a runtime.
styled-components: A React-specific CSS-in-JS library that allows CSS to be written inline with component definitions, producing hashed class names at runtime. Introduced in 2016; typical runtime cost is around 25 KB plus a Babel transform.
Emotion: A CSS-in-JS library for React, similar in concept to styled-components. Offers template-string and object-syntax styling with runtime-generated class names.
BEM: Block, Element, Modifier. A CSS naming convention developed at Yandex that encodes component structure into class names (for example, 'product-card__heading--featured'). An attempt to fake scoping via naming discipline in the absence of native scope support.
Integrated by Design: First book by Vivian Voss, published by Voss'scher Verlag on 23 April 2026 (UNESCO World Book Day). 371 pages, full colour. Subtitle: 'Why the Best Systems Are the Ones You Don't Notice'. FreeBSD from philosophy to practice, in four parts and 22 chapters.
KDP: Kindle Direct Publishing. Amazon's self-publishing platform for paperback, hardcover, and Kindle editions. Handles printing, distribution, royalty calculation, and marketplace listings. Notorious among first-time European publishers for a pricing form that accepts what it calls 'list price' and treats it as net, adding local book VAT on top at checkout.
JetBrains Mono: A free and open-source monospaced programming font released by JetBrains in 2020. Ships in sixteen styles across four weights with matching italics. Designed for long reading sessions at small sizes, with tall lowercase and open counters. Distributed under the SIL Open Font Licence 1.1, which permits renamed derivatives.
Counter (typography): The enclosed or partially enclosed blank space inside a letterform, such as the two openings in the numeral 8 or the opening in a lowercase 'o'.
